stem: Solve the initial value problem \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = 0 \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \), and evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \).
solution: The characteristic roots are \( [[p]] \) and \( [[q]] \), so \( y = C_1 e^{[[p]]t} + C_2 e^{[[q]]t} \). The conditions \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \) give \( C_1 = [[c1]] \) and \( C_2 = [[c2]] \). Hence \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps the two constants \( C_1 \) and \( C_2 \).
This splits the initial value equally between the modes, ignoring \( y'(0) \).
This keeps only the first exponential mode.
This keeps only the second exponential mode.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Solve the initial value problem \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = 0 \), where the characteristic equation has a REPEATED root, given \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) and \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \). Evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \).
solution: The characteristic equation has a repeated root \( r = [[r]] \), so the general solution is \( y = (C_1 + C_2 x)e^{[[r]]x} \). Then \( y(0) = C_1 = [[y0]] \), and \( y'(x) = C_2 e^{[[r]]x} + [[r]](C_1+C_2 x)e^{[[r]]x} \) gives \( y'(0) = C_2 + [[r]]C_1 = [[v0]] \), so \( C_2 = [[c2]] \). Hence \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract \( [[r]]y_0 \) when solving for \( C_2 \), using \( v_0 \) directly.
This uses \( e^{-[[r]]x} \) instead of the correct \( e^{[[r]]x} \).
This drops the polynomial correction term entirely, as if the root were not repeated.
This multiplies the wrong constant by \( x \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Solve the initial value problem \( y'' + ([[c]])y = 0 \) (the characteristic roots are the purely imaginary pair \( \pm [[w]]i \)), given \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) and \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \). Evaluate \( y\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2\cdot[[w]]}\right) \).
solution: With roots \( \pm [[w]]i \), the general solution is \( y = y_0\cos([[w]]x) + \dfrac{v_0}{[[w]]}\sin([[w]]x) \). At \( x = \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2\cdot[[w]]} \), \( [[w]]x = \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2} \), so \( y = [[y0]]\cos\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2}\right) + \dfrac{[[v0]]}{[[w]]}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps the roles of sine and cosine.
This forgets to divide the velocity term by \( [[w]] \).
This uses the wrong sign on the sine term.
This leaves an extra factor of \( [[w]] \) inside the trig arguments instead of letting it cancel.
This drops the velocity term entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The nonhomogeneous equation \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[d]] \) admits a constant particular solution. Solve the initial value problem with \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \), and evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \).
solution: A constant particular solution \( y_p = [[yp]] \) satisfies \( ([[c]])y_p = [[d]] \). Writing \( y = y_p + z \), \( z \) solves the homogeneous equation with \( z(0) = [[y0]]-[[yp]] \), \( z'(0) = [[v0]] \), giving \( C_1 = [[c1]] \), \( C_2 = [[c2]] \). Hence \( y([[t0]]) = C_1e^{[[p]]\cdot[[t0]]} + C_2e^{[[q]]\cdot[[t0]]} + [[yp]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to add back the particular solution \( y_p \).
This uses \( y_0 \) directly instead of shifting by \( y_p \) before solving for the constants.
This swaps the two homogeneous constants \( C_1 \) and \( C_2 \).
This drops the second exponential mode entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The forced equation \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = [[d]]e^{[[r]]x} \) has a particular solution of the form \( Ae^{[[r]]x} \) (the forcing rate \( [[r]] \) is not a characteristic root). Given \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) and \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \), evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \).
solution: Try \( y_p = Ae^{[[r]]x} \): substituting gives \( A = \dfrac{[[d]]}{([[r]]-([[p]]))([[r]]-([[q]]))} = [[A]] \). The homogeneous part must absorb the LEFTOVER initial data \( z(0) = [[y0]] - [[A]] = [[yshift]] \), \( z'(0) = [[v0]] - [[A]]\cdot[[r]] = [[vshift]] \) (since \( y_p \) itself already contributes to both \( y(0) \) and \( y'(0) \)), giving \( C_1 = [[c1]] \), \( C_2 = [[c2]] \). Hence \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the exponential factor on the particular-solution term.
This solves for \( C_1,C_2 \) using the raw \( y_0,v_0 \), forgetting that the particular solution also contributes to \( y'(0) \).
This swaps the two homogeneous constants \( C_1 \) and \( C_2 \).
This uses \( [[d]] \) itself as the particular amplitude, ignoring the denominator.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A block of mass \(1\) kg on a frictionless spring with spring constant \([[wsq]]\) N/m has displacement \(x(t)\) satisfying \(x'' + [[wsq]]x = 0\). It starts at \(x(0) = [[y0]]\) m with velocity \(x'(0) = [[v0]]\) m/s. Find its velocity \(x'\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2\cdot[[w]]}\right)\) (in m/s).
solution: The position is \( x(t) = [[y0]]\cos([[w]]t) + \dfrac{[[v0]]}{[[w]]}\sin([[w]]t) \), so the velocity is \( x'(t) = -[[y0]]\cdot[[w]]\sin([[w]]t) + [[v0]]\cos([[w]]t) \). At \( t = \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2\cdot[[w]]} \), \( [[w]]t = \dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2} \), so \( x' = -[[y0]]\cdot[[w]]\sin\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2}\right) + [[v0]]\cos\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign on the sine term.
This computes the POSITION \( x(t) \) rather than the requested velocity \( x'(t) \).
This forgets to multiply the sine term by \( [[w]] \) and instead multiplies the cosine term.
This differentiates the wrong way, keeping cosine with \([[y0]]\cdot[[w]]\) instead of sine.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
